name: Robotics Toolbox for Python (RTB-P)

performance.notes: NumPy-based; fast enough for offline analysis; not real-time control

agent_readiness.notes: Python port of MATLAB Robotics Toolbox. import roboticstoolbox as rtb. robot = rtb.models.Panda(). T = robot.fkine(robot.qz). sol = robot.ikine_LM(T). J = robot.jacobe(robot.qr). traj = rtb.jtraj(robot.qz, robot.qr, 50). q, qd, qdd = traj.q, traj.qd, traj.qdd. robot.plot(q). SE3: from spatialmath import SE3. T = SE3.Trans(1,0,0) * SE3.Ry(np.pi/4). DHRobot with DH parameters. Swift visualizer. spatialmath companion library. Built-in robots: Panda, UR5, Sawyer, Fetch.

gotchas: spatialmath library required for SE3/SO3 types
IK solvers are iterative — can fail or be slow for degenerate configurations
Not suitable for real-time control (use Pinocchio for that)
MATLAB Robotics Toolbox users will find familiar patterns but different syntax
Swift visualizer requires additional setup in Jupyter
best_when: Teaching and learning robot kinematics and dynamics
Rapid prototyping of robot trajectories and motion planning
When MATLAB Robotics Toolbox concepts are familiar
avoid_when: Real-time robot control (use Pinocchio or robot vendor SDK)
summary: Robotics Toolbox for Python (RTB-P) is the Python port of Corke's MATLAB toolbox. rtb.models.Panda(). robot.fkine(q). robot.ikine_LM(T). robot.jacobe(q). jtraj() for joint trajectories. Swift visualizer. Built-in UR5, Panda, Sawyer. SE3 via spatialmath. MIT by Peter Corke/QUT. Best for teaching and rapid kinematics prototyping. Not real-time. IK can fail for degenerate configs. Requires spatialmath.
